subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The entire chapter is organised around discovering and using area formulas for triangles (section 7.2), parallelograms (section 7.4), rhombuses (section 7.5), and trapeziums (section 7.6), and develops strategies for composite 2D shapes via triangle decomposition (section 7.3). Forty exercises apply and extend these formulas across composite figures, Śulba-Sūtra transformations, and real-life contexts.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Area derivations in sections 7.2, 7.4, and 7.5 depend directly on properties of triangles (RHS congruence), parallelograms (equal opposite sides), and rhombuses (perpendicular bisecting diagonals). These properties are not just recalled — they drive each dissection proof.

The chapter contains explicit deductive proofs: the universal triangle area formula via RHS (section 7.2), the parallelogram dissection proof (section 7.4), three independent derivations of the trapezium formula (section 7.6), and the mirror-reflection minimum-perimeter argument (section 7.2). Students are also asked to complete and produce dissection arguments in multiple exercises.

Śulba-Sūtras (ancient Indian geometric texts) are explicitly cited in seven exercises across sections 7.2, 7.4, and 7.6 (E-10, E-11, E-25, E-26, E-27, E-35, and the rhombus dissection in section 7.5), positioning Indian mathematical heritage as a primary framework for equal-area transformation problems rather than a passing cultural note.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to derive and apply area formulas for triangles, parallelograms, rhombuses, and trapeziums by using dissection — cutting and rearranging figures into rectangles of equal area — alongside deductive geometric reasoning.
